# AI Thread

- [https://chatgpt.com/c/697771fb-a1ec-832c-aa14-ea46f5e41d46](https://chatgpt.com/c/697771fb-a1ec-832c-aa14-ea46f5e41d46) \- good one on “why it exists” that combines problem/solution

# PDP Core Persuasive Components

* ATF Section  
  * Hero Carousel  
    * Hero Video  
    * Mirror Content Sections  
  * Copy Block  
    * Headline  
    * Paragraph  
    * Bullets  
    * Icons  
* BTF Sections  
  * CTA Box  
    * Quantity options (e.g. 1/3/6 or 1/2/3)  
    * S\&S options (e.g. ships every 30/60/90 days)  
  * Content Sections  
    * Product Highlights (from Curated FAQ)  
    * Video UGC Testimonial Carousel (Social Proof)  
    * What’s in It Hero (Ingredients)  
    * What to Expect Timeline (Benefits)  
    * The Problem (Agitation)  
    * The Solution (USP)  
    * Our Experts (Credibility)  
    * Why it Works (Logic)  
    * Comparison Chart (Advantages)  
    * Cost Savings Chart (Affordability)  
    * How it’s Made (Quality)  
    * How to Use (Instructions)  
    * Reviews & Ratings (Social Proof)  
    * FAQ (Objections)  
* Miscellaneous  
  * PDP  
  * Collapsed Benefit Summary

Other Key Components

- Header  
  * Hamburger Menu  
  * Logo  
  * Search  
  * Cart Icon  
- Promo bar  
  * Feature key things – a sale, special promotion, free shipping, etc.  
- Navigation  
- Sticky CTA

# Definitions

- Key Selling Points (KSPs) – the most salient, outcome-driven reasons to buy the product, expressed as fast-scannable claims that answer why this product is worth choosing right now versus doing nothing or buying an alternative.

# Strategy

To create the “perfect” PDP with all the necessary component to mix and match placement, existence and to re-use in other areas like the VSL CTA reveal view, e-comm DR longer-form landers, in-feed VSL landers, etc.

# Foundational Principles

The proposed “perfect” PDP is founded on the following best practice principles and assumptions:

- **DR Principles** – Everything is centered on traditional DR principles of persuasion, but re-mixed into the hunt-and-peck type of behavior seen on PDPs  
- **Semi-Qualified Traffic** – The assumption is most of the traffic is somewhat warm already, mostly having come from product-first ads and organic searches that show intent; however, the page also has to speak to those coming from shopping and search where the may have been doing category searches and don’t know much about the specific product yet.  
- **UX/UI Best Practices** – Leverages much of the research from Baymard, Norman Nielsen and other sources who have run usability and conversion testing to tease out the specifics  
- **Scannability** – The page is easily scannable so people can pick and choose what areas they want to focus on to answer their questions and alleviate their concerns

 

# ATF \+ CTA Section

## Hero Thumbnail Carousel

- Purpose  
  * Quickly deliver main KSPs via engaging visuals  
- Strategy  
  * Tell the story visually, quickly, just like it was a PDP on Amazon  
- Implementation  
  * Select top 10 KSPs once PDP has been developed, shrink down as necessary and turn into engaging visuals  
  * Include at least one product video in the thumbnail, ideally as the first thumbnail, i.e. the visual is powerful, but it’s overlayed with a play button and triggers a 30 highlight reel with the best KSPs  
- Examples  
  * [https://us.foursigmatic.com/products/think-ground-coffee-bag](https://us.foursigmatic.com/products/think-ground-coffee-bag)  
  * [https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee](https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee)  
- Best Practices  
  * General  
    * High fidelity to bring things to life  
    * Product in action  
    * Lifestyle imagery  
    * Delicious/taste/beauty, etc. to bring it alive where possible  
  * 10 thumbnails that follow story architecture  
  * Show first 5 beneath the first one, large enough to act as teaser  
  * Easily seen arrows on either side of either the hero or the mini thumbs beneath the hero

![][image1] ![][image2]

### Thumbnail 1 – Outcome (Why Am I Here Again?)

- **What It Shows** – Clean product shot paired with the primary outcome promise, with a play button overlay that opens a 30-second "best of" overview video.  
- **Why It Exists** – Reconfirms the exact promise the user was pre-sold on and offers the fastest path to re-belief.  
- **Example Copy** – “Calm Focus. All Day.” or “Steady Energy. No Crash.”  
- **Example Visual** – Bottle or pouch centered on a neutral or lifestyle background, bold outcome headline, subtle play icon similar to Amazon PDP behavior.  
- **Live Example** – [https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes](https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes) (thumbnail 1\) \- this doesn’t have the video overlay on it though

![][image3]

### Thumbnail 2 – Problem Signs (Is this for Me?)

* **What It Shows** – A visual checklist or ring of common symptoms, behaviors, or frustrations that signal the user may need this product.

* **Why It Exists** – Creates instant self-identification and relevance before asking the user to evaluate ingredients or mechanisms.

* **Example Copy** – “Low Energy” | “Brain Fog” | “Jitters” | “Crash Later” | “Gut Issues.”

* **Example Visual** – Lifestyle image with labeled callouts or badges around the subject highlighting recognizable problem signs.

* **Live Example** – [https://thepetlabco.com/products/probiotic-chews-dogs/optexp/whrfnd2](https://thepetlabco.com/products/probiotic-chews-dogs/optexp/whrfnd2) (thumbnail 5\)

![][image4]

### Thumbnail 3 – Ingredients (What’s in It?)

- **What It Shows** – The product image centered, surrounded by key ingredient visuals placed around the product in a clear circular layout, each paired with a 1–2 word caveman-style benefit.  
- **Why It Exists** – Answers “what’s in this” in human terms and makes ingredients feel real and intentional.  
- **Example Copy** – “Lion’s Mane – Focus” | “Cordyceps – Stamina” | “Reishi – Calm” | “Turkey Tail – Gut” | “Shiitake – Immune.”  
- **Example Visual** – Product centered with ingredient photography or illustrations orbiting around it, subtle connectors pointing inward, benefit words directly under each ingredient.  
- **Live Example** – [https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee](https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee) (thumbnail 2\)

![][image5]

### Thumbnail 4 – Solution Enjoyment (What Do I Enjoy?)

- **What It Shows** – Real-life usage moments that communicate enjoyment, ease, and lifestyle integration.  
- **Why It Exists** – Builds desire by showing the lived experience of using the product, not just the outcome or claim.  
- **Example Copy** – “Feels Good Every Day.” or “Easy Morning Ritual.”  
- **Example Visual** – Lifestyle imagery of a real person enjoying the product in a natural setting, optionally with a subtle play overlay.  
- **Live Example** – [https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes](https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes) (thumbnail 3\)

![][image6]

### Thumbnail 5 – How it Works (Why It Makes Sense)

- **What It Shows** – A clear 4-step flow that links a relatable problem to a specific ingredient, the biological mechanism it affects, and the felt benefit that results.  
- **Why It Exists** – Makes the solution feel logical and earned by showing a straight line from relatable problem → ingredient → solution mechanism → simple felt benefit.  
- **Example Copy** – “Stress Spike → L-theanine → Boosts calming alpha waves → Calm Focus.”  
- **Example Visual** – Four simple panels or arrows in sequence, each labeled with one short phrase and supported by a minimal icon.  
- **Live Example** – [https://trycreate.co/products/sour-peach-core-create-creatine-monohydrate-gummies](https://trycreate.co/products/sour-peach-core-create-creatine-monohydrate-gummies) (thumbnail 6\)

![][image7]

### Thumbnail 6 – What to Expect (When Will I Notice?)

**What It Shows** – A simple 3-phase timeline showing how results typically unfold over time.

- **Why It Exists** – Sets expectations, reframes subtle early signals as progress, and prevents premature doubt.  
- **Example Copy** – “Days 1–3: Feel Subtle.” → “Week 1–2: Feel Steady.” → “Week 3+: Feel Change.”  
- **Example Visual** – Timeline with large time markers, short caveman labels, calm non-hype styling.  
- **Live Example** – [https://primalqueen.com](https://primalqueen.com) (in body) – couldn’t find a perfect example yet, so below is decent framework, however, ideally this is simplified, less copy, more visuals to show what’s happening inside or to them, but generally having that line to the left with the checkmarks gives it that timeline, and then you anchor it to specific milestones with an immediate, medium and long-term focus as more than 3 isn’t necessary, so 1 week, 1 month, 1 year (or 3 or 6 months)

![][image8]

### Thumbnail 7 – How to Use (Is this Easy?)

- **What It Shows** – A simple 3-step instruction overview covering dose, timing, and method.  
- **Why It Exists** – Removes friction by making the habit feel easy and obvious on mobile.  
- **Example Copy** – “2 Pills.” → “Morning.” → “With Water.”  
- **Example Visual** – Numbered steps with large numerals: capsules next to bottle, sun or moon icon, glass-of-water icon.  
- **Live Example** – [https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes](https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes) (thumbnail 7\) and [https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee](https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee) (thumbnail 4\)

![][image9] ![][image10]

### Thumbnail 8 – Believability / Credibility (Where’s the Proof?)

- **What It Shows** – One strong credibility anchor such as a study reference, expert endorsement, or substantiated claim.  
- **Why It Exists** – Signals seriousness and legitimacy, answering “why should I believe this works.”  
- **Example Copy** – “Clinically Studied Ingredients” or “Doctor Formulated.”  
- **Example Visual** – Medical professional in neutral setting, study citation graphic, or authoritative badge.  
- **Live Example** – [https://im8health.com/products/essentials](https://im8health.com/products/essentials) (thumbnail 3\) and [https://drinkag1.com/products/greens-powder-pouch](https://drinkag1.com/products/greens-powder-pouch) (thumbnail 7\)

![][image11] ![][image12]

### Thumbnail 9 – Alternative Comparison (Why is This Better?)

- **What It Shows** – A simple comparison table with our product highlighted versus typical alternatives, capped at 5 rows.  
- **Why It Exists** – Collapses the choice into an obvious verdict and reduces comparison shopping.  
- **Example Copy** – Rows like “No Jitters” | “Better Mood” | “Gut Support” | “Clean Ingredients” | “No Added Sugar.”  
- **Example Visual** – Three-column table: Feature, Our Product, Typical Alternative, with checks and Xs and our column visually emphasized.  
- **Live Example** – [https://us.foursigmatic.com/products/think-ground-coffee-bag](https://us.foursigmatic.com/products/think-ground-coffee-bag) (in body)

![][image13]

### Thumbnail 10 – Testimonials (Emotional Close)

- **What It Shows** – A real user mid-use with a play overlay that opens a fast-cut testimonial montage.  
- **Why It Exists** – Transfers belief through social proof and closes the loop emotionally with FOMO.  
- **Example Copy** – “This Changed My Life.” or “I Finally Feel Normal Again.”  
- **Example Visual** – Authentic lifestyle shot of a happy customer using the product, captioned and overlaid with a play icon that opens a 20–30 second testimonial video.  
- **Live Example** – [https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee](https://www.ryzesuperfoods.com/products/mushroom-coffee) (thumbnail 6\) \- note: this doesn’t perfectly represent the above vision; ideally there would be a single person close up with a power quote underneath that has the video play button overlaid, and when clicked, brings that video testimonial to life and then quick cuts into 30 seconds or so of additional quick-cut, high-energy/enthusiasm video testimonials… that higher energy, OMG factor is what creates FOMO, which is what this is all about

![][image14]

## Below the Thumbnails – Facts Panel (What Exactly is in it?)

- **What It Shows** – The full Nutrition Facts or Supplement Facts panel on the left, paired with a vertical stack of key quality and diet icons on the right.  
- **Why It Exists** – Gives analytical buyers immediate permission to verify while reassuring casual shoppers with scannable quality cues.  
- **Why Its Here** – The nutrition/supplement facts are extremely important to a very particular set of users and we don’t want them to have to hunt for the info; placing it here gives it prominent visibility as it acts like a caption  
- **Example Copy** – “Full Supplement Facts” paired with icon labels like “Vegan,” “Gluten Free,” “No Added Sugar,” “Dairy Free,” “Soy Free.”  
- **Example Visual** – Clean facts panel occupying the left two-thirds of the frame, right-hand column of minimal quality icons, no bottom badges or extra callouts.  
- **Live Example** – TBD

## Product Block

- TBD  
- Quality icons  
- Examples  
  * [https://thepetlabco.com/products/probiotic-chews-dogs/optexp/whrfnd2](https://thepetlabco.com/products/probiotic-chews-dogs/optexp/whrfnd2) \- their CTA box is pretty clean, nice toggle

## CTA / Offer Box

- Include the free shipping, guarantee, fast shipping, etc. bar beneath the CTA

# BTF Section

## Template

- **What It Shows** –   
- **Why It Exists** –   
- **Best Practices** –   
  *   
- **Example(s)** –   
  * 

## Product Highlights (TLDR)

- **What It Shows** – Digestible caveman bits of copy that address the top 3-5 questions / issues upfront, at a quick glance  
- **Why It Exists** – To resolve major objections as fast as possible for those who scroll beneath the CTA to re-confirm, or discover in the first place, what the product is about  
- **Best Practices** – Choose the top 3-5 questions or issues people have, e.g. what does it do, what’s in it, why is it difference, what about shipping, returns, etc.?  Think of them like mental blockers that, until resolved, will block purchase.  Here’s a good framework that can be customized based on product type:  
  * **What’s in It** – Show supplement / nutrition facts panel  
  * **How it Helps** – 5-7 short benefit bullets with microscripts  
  * **Why it Works –** 1 sentence crystal clear delivery of the problem mechanism followed by a simple graphic dramatizing how it works in the body, ideally re-used from the thumbnail section  
  * **Shipping & Returns** – Emphasize free S\&H if available and expected shipping speed   
  * **Our Guarantee** – simple summary of the guarantee, emphasizing the key X benefit to be experienced within Y days (whatever the guarantee period is).  
- **Good Example** – [https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes](https://www.kachava.com/products/shakes)    
  * Shows 5 key points their specific buyers want to know about, mixing in KSPs with typical buyer objections like quality, shipping, etc.  
  * Mixes in hard data (e.g. a nutrition facts panel) with good copy (e.g. in the Unbeatable Taste) section  
  * Bring in some personality to it, not just boring facts

![][image15]

- **Bad Example** – [https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/longevity-blend-multinutrient-drink-mix-blood-orange-flavor](https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/longevity-blend-multinutrient-drink-mix-blood-orange-flavor)   
  * Boring labels   
  * Missing shipping/returns

![][image16]

## UGC Video Carousel (Social Proof) \- DONE

- **What It Shows** – 5-7 energetic UGC video testimonials  
- **Why It Exists** – Provide social proof and FOMO that the product actually works  
- **Best Practices**  
  * Curate the testimonials to be the best of the best, with engaging people who can deliver their energy and enthusiasm through the screen  
  * Ideally select a variety of avatars / personas to display for max coverage  
  * Great to re-use UGC ads that did well, just strip out the selling  
  * But best to get real customers to shoot with phone, up close  
  * Keep all of the weirdness and ums and uhs, keep it real  
  * Can mix in also videos from experts, just keep them authentic  
  * Have arrows on either side of the carousel  
  * The video should be playing on mute in the background with some clear signal to click to hear it  
  * If user navigates to next video, stop previous video  
- **Example(s)**  
  * [https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-brain](https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-brain)  
    * Best example of most best practices integrated  
    * Very authentic, leads with real testimonials  
    * Mixes in influencer content  
    * Highly visible nav arrows\!  
      ![][image17]  
  * [https://shop.upwellness.com/pages/golden-revive-plus](https://shop.upwellness.com/pages/golden-revive-plus)  
    * 100% UGC, extremely authentic  
    * Great diversity of testimonials  
    * Clear play button  
    * Shows partial video to right to indicate there’s more  
    * Just missing clear nav arrows on either side of video  
      ![][image18]  
  * [https://trycreate.co/products/sour-peach-core-create-creatine-monohydrate-gummies](https://trycreate.co/products/sour-peach-core-create-creatine-monohydrate-gummies)  
    * Video-only style  
    * Great UGC vibe, engaging people  
    * Highly visible nav arrows\!

![][image19]

* [https://simplynootropics.com/products/nmn-powder](https://simplynootropics.com/products/nmn-powder)  
  * Great combo of video and written  
    * Feels slightly less authentic though

  ![][image20]

- https://shop.upwellness.com/pages/golden-revive-plus

## The Expert (Credibility) – TBD

- **What It Shows** – Credible image and/or video of whatever expert either created the product or endorses the product  
- **Why It Exists** – To provide the credibility needed to overcome skepticism and anxiety as to whether the product is safe and effective  
- **Best Practices**  
  * Ideally use a video, with thumbnail of them in act of speaking  
  * Always put them in white coat where possible  
  * Have their full title and ideally where they went to school, e.g. XX School of Medicine  
  * Have them speak naturally, no canned scripts, just splice different takes if necessary, or ask them prompting questions to get natural answers  
  * Show captions like it was a UGC ad  
  * When user scrolls away, the video should stop playing  
- **Example(s)** –   
  * [https://humann.com/products/superbeets-soft-chew](https://humann.com/products/superbeets-soft-chew)  
    * Excellent execution of video  
    * Uses two experts for different perspectives  
      ![][image21]  
  * [https://trycreate.co/products/sour-peach-core-create-creatine-monohydrate-gummies](https://trycreate.co/products/sour-peach-core-create-creatine-monohydrate-gummies)  
    * Uses images instead of video  
    * Credentials are great, but they miss an opportunity to bring it to life via what they have to say about the product

![][image22]

## What’s in It (Ingredients) \- FINISH

- **What It Shows** –   
- **Why It Exists** –   
- **Best Practices** –   
  * **Compelling Visual** – Begin with a compelling visual that brings the ingredient to life, e.g. a beautiful flower the ingredient is sourced from; if no compelling visual, can get creative with molecular structures, different colored powders with different textures, etc.  
  * **Ingredient Name \+ Microscript Benefit** – Combine the ingredient name \+ the microscript benefit, e.g. “L-Theanine for Tranquility”  
  * **Description** – Succinctly 1-2 sentence explanation on what it the ingredient is and how that ingredient delivers the benefit, i.e. tie together the benefit with the mechanism  
  * **List Vertically and Expanded** – Show these vertically and expanded, do not roll up accordion-style unless there are many ingredients; if you do roll-up, show a teaser of the content with the first ingredient expanded already to signal that’s how the rest work… and if someone expands one section, it should collapse the other  
- **Example(s)** –   
  * [https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint](https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint)  
    * Although this is horizontal slider, it’s well executed, so imagine this done vertically instead  
    * Has beautiful imagery that brings each ingredient to life with variety  
    * Good ingredient descriptions, succinct but powerful  
    * Missing benefit in ingredient name though  
    * Body font too small

![][image23]

* [https://healthybonesco.com/products/collagen-peptides](https://healthybonesco.com/products/collagen-peptides)   
  * Good example of vertical accordion style  
    * Shows first entry expanded already (although their UX/UI implementation is a bit buggy)  
    * Has the clean plus signs and the X sign to collapse content (although that’s buggy too)  
    * Labels are benefit-first, which is good, but it should have the ingredient in that label too in the X for Y format, e.g. Fortibone for Bone Density Support

    

    ![][image24]

## What to Expect (Benefits) \- DONE

- **What It Shows** – A step by step timeline of when and how the ingredients will affect them  
- **Why It Exists** – To future pace them, showing them the real, tangible benefits and payoff of continuing to take it, helping them visualize how their underlying biochemistry is changing, increasing believability   
- **Best Practices** –   
  * **Cover Key Time Frames** – It should have 3-5 key timeframes, short, medium and long term at a minimum; ideally, it’s 7 days, 2 weeks, 1 Month, 3 Months and 6 months  
  * **Vertical Line on Left** – This gives it a timeline feel, ideally that line appears gradually as the user scrolls with a big circle to the left of each milestone  
  * **Benefit Title** – The title for each milestone should be a powerful benefit microscript  
  * **Description** – Short explanation that connects the dots to what the ingredient is doing in the body as it builds up and what benefits they should enjoy  
  * **Optional Image** – Can also add an image that dimensionless the benefits to the user, i.e. show a user doing something exciting, smiling, lifestyle imagery, etc. without being cheesy  
- **Example(s)** – No examples are perfect, so use guidelines above combined with inspiration from below:  
  * [https://seed.com/daily-synbiotic](https://seed.com/daily-synbiotic)  
    * Nice milestone labels that pop  
    * Great benefit headlines for each milestone  
    * Bullets, while easy to read, are kind of generic, probably for compliance  
    * Make the circles bigger and have the line unfold as user scrolls  
    * Optionally add in lifestyle imagery for each milestone

    

    ![][image25]

  * [https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint](https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint)  
    * Great execution of open circles on left with border a dark color and then inside a lighter color, which then matches the label  
    * The descriptions nicely progress someone through what they should be feeling, although missing mechanism and a bit word inefficient  
    * Timeline doesn’t unfold when scrolling, which gives it that timeline feel

    ![][image26]

  * [https://superfoodscompany.com/products/superfood-tabs](https://superfoodscompany.com/products/superfood-tabs)  
    * This is a creative one that used auto-playing simple images to dramatize the transformation the woman goes through  
    * Missing a bunch of best practices, but this is a nice feature that brings to life the transformation they are going through

  ![][image27]

## The Problem (Agitation) – TBD (may consolidate)

- [https://mengotomars.com/](https://mengotomars.com/)  
- https://www.nutreance.com/products/redimove

## The Solution (USP) – TBD (may consolidate)

- [https://mengotomars.com/](https://mengotomars.com/)  
- [https://www.nutreance.com/products/redimove](https://www.nutreance.com/products/redimove)   
  * I like how it has 3 sections that 1\) create paradigm shift (i.e. it’s NOT what you think), 2\) reveals the real cause (it’s actually Y) and 3\) introduces the new solution  
- [https://www.nutreance.com/products/glucoven](https://www.nutreance.com/products/glucoven)  
  * Repeat of the above, same rhythm  
  * Aha, just realized these are shopping links, so there’s really limited info they’ve had before unlike a product-first video ad, so, like search ads, the shopping ads may want to point to a customized lander where more of the problem/solution/rationale is featured… would be an awesome split test

## Why it Works (Logic) – TBD (may consolidate)

- **What It Shows** – Dramatizes how the formula works from a biochemical / mechanistic perspective, like a simple biochem tutorial that’s conveys the underlying reason why it works  
- **Why It Exists** – Provides the logical answer to how it works, so people can check that off the list  
- **Best Practices** –   
  *   
- **Example(s)** –   
  * [https://pendulumlife.com/pages/shop-pendulum-glucose-control-probiotic-blood-sugar-support](https://pendulumlife.com/pages/shop-pendulum-glucose-control-probiotic-blood-sugar-support)  
    * Look at biochem diagram  
    * Not the best but it’ll do

## Comparison Chart (Advantages) \- DONE

- **What It Shows** – A simple comparison of the product vs. the primary comparison the prospect would do, which is usually “Other Products”, but can be specific  
- **Why It Exists** – Visually show the superiority of the product vs. the competition with the key features / elements listed  
- **Best Practices** –   
  * Keep it to 3 simple columns, the first one with no label, second with product name, third one for the comparison (don’t do 3-4 comparison columns, too noisy, only do more than 1 if a super competitive commoditized market with many similar competitors and extremely well known brand names)  
  * Each row is a critical feature/advantage/benefit of the product, covering all major things people care about, e.g. if protein, is it Grass-fed, if it’s a beauty topical, is it “Free from X”, etc.  
  * Use primary/recency effect to show the most important comparisons first and then last and least ones in between  
  * Ideally the value for each row is a simple check box for the product whereas the comparison will be an X, thus clearly positioning that product as superior  
    * The value can also be a quantitative measurement or a description where it makes sense, but ideally stick to one style with only minimal mixing where absolutely needed, e.g. calories per serving  
  * Keep to 5-7 for ease of digestibility  
  * Can optionally include an icon to the left of the feature/advantage/benefit that dramatizes it  
  * For the product column (2nd one) can also highlight the entire column with a colored background make it pop, especially if more than 1 comparison  
- **Example(s)** –   
  * [https://us.foursigmatic.com/products/think-ground-coffee-bag](https://us.foursigmatic.com/products/think-ground-coffee-bag)  
    * Near ideal example of best practices listed  
    * Limits to one comparison, the generic category of regular coffee  
    * Mixes the attributes between presence of something positive and absence of something negative, e.g. “No Jitters” vs. “Positive Mood” so that everything is a checkbox  
    * Also mixes in words where needed, e.g. coffee quality and 100% real mushrooms, leveraging primary and recency, especially the primary USP which appears to be the “fruiting body”  
    * Only thing to change would be the color, the reverse text (white on color) can be hard to read so would change that to a lighter background against which normal black text pops like in Instant Hydration example below

  ![][image28]


  * [https://instanthydration.com/products/premium-electrolyte-drink-mix](https://instanthydration.com/products/premium-electrolyte-drink-mix)   
    * Super competitive market with very well known brand names, so they went with 2 comparisons, which is appropriate here  
    * Nice highlighting of the product column  
    * Only major issue is they don’t start with any truly differentiating feature; the first four simply state it’s basically the same as LMNT; I would’ve gone with something like “Full Spectrum Electrolytes” since it has calcium whereas the others don’t

    ![][image29]

  * Xx  
  * 

## Cost Savings Chart (Affordability) – Done

- **What It Shows** – Side-by-side comparison of cost of buying each individual ingredient vs. how much the all-in-one product costs  
- **Why It Exists** – To dramatize the value and cost savings easily  
- **Best Practices** –   
  * 3 columns  
    * First is name of ingredient  
    * Second is for the product with checkboxes as values with the price of the product at the bottom  
    * Third is price if bought individually, so “Standalone Price” or something similar, with the price as the values and the total at the bottom  
  * Centered at the bottom should some way of dramatizing how much is saved, e.g. “You Save $49” in a styled starbust or rectangle  
- **Example(s)** –   
  * [https://mudwtr.com/products/coffee-starter-kit](https://mudwtr.com/products/coffee-starter-kit)  
    * Instead of each ingredient showing, it shows the benefits, which is smart idea  
    * Just missing the punchline at the end of “Saves You $146” as always need to do the math for people to reduce cognitive load

![][image30]

## How it’s Made (Quality) \- TBD

## How to Use (Instructions) \- TBD

## Reviews & Ratings (Social Proof) – Done

- **What It Shows** – Amazon-like user ratings with individual user reviews beneath  
- **Why It Exists** – People mostly look for “how bad is it” vs. “how good is it”; they know negative reviews exist and want to confirm the downside is limited  
- **Best Practices** –   
  * Start with the full Amazon-style user ratings break down with horizontal bars as people want to see the general distribution and not just the absolute rating  
  * Show a curated UGC strip of photos that, when clicked, trigger a popover that has that user’s full review, along with left/right navigation arrows to browse others  
  * That’s followed by the individual user ratings  
  * Ensure the ratings section isn’t overall shrunken down into a small font as can happen when plug-ins are used with no customization; it should be precisely the same style as everything else and fit it or else it becomes hard to read.  
- **Example(s)** –   
  * [https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint](https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint)  
    * Has the Amazon-style breakdown  
    * Has the image carousel that when clicked, triggers browsable popover (see 2nd screenshot) with the image \+ written review that pairs with it  
    * Formatted nicely, seamless with rest of page

![][image31]

![][image32]

- **What It Shows** –   
- **Why It Exists** –   
- **Best Practices** –

## Other – TBD

- Go back and add “Why it’s Here” for rationale about location  
- Add overall pillars in a bigging section, the foundational principles upon which this is based  
  * Grounded in DR principles, usability research and CRO UX/UI design best practices  
  * Targets the warm user who is pre-sold by an ad or a referral, and a such, re-orders the natural way a long-form page would show  
  * Focuses on scanability and digestibility  
- Benefits Hero  
  * Like at [https://javvycoffee.com/products/protein-coffee](https://javvycoffee.com/products/protein-coffee), look towards top, it’s product shot with benefits underneath with imagery  
- BTF CTA x-sells  
  * [https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/longevity-blend-multinutrient-drink-mix-blood-orange-flavor](https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/longevity-blend-multinutrient-drink-mix-blood-orange-flavor)   
- Credibility strip  
  * Likely right beneath the offer box  
    * [https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/longevity-blend-multinutrient-drink-mix-blood-orange-flavor](https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/longevity-blend-multinutrient-drink-mix-blood-orange-flavor)   
- Identity Matching \- who is this good for

  * What: A 3-column section showing different use cases.

    * Image 1 (Gym Rat): "For Strength & Reps."

    * Image 2 (Professional): "For Focus & Cognition."

    * Image 3 (Wellness/Longevity): "For Recovery & Vitality."

  * Why: Broadens the market. Creatine isn't just for bodybuilders anymore. This tells the "warm" visitor who saw a lifestyle ad, "Yes, this is for you, even if you don't lift heavy weights."

- The Proof

  * [https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint](https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint)

    * Big circles with key data points in it, could be from studies, a survey, a graph, etc.

    * Consider integrating with the solution for efficiency

  * [https://pendulumlife.com/pages/shop-pendulum-glucose-control-probiotic-blood-sugar-support](https://pendulumlife.com/pages/shop-pendulum-glucose-control-probiotic-blood-sugar-support)

    * See the graph BTF

  * [https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint](https://stonehengehealth.com/products/dynamic-joint)

# Best Practices Research

**Comprehensive Guide to Mobile PDP Best Practices**

**Based on Research by Baymard Institute & Nielsen Norman Group (NN/g)**

**Introduction: The "Verticality & Discovery" Mandate**

Mobile users operate in a ruthless environment of high distraction and low patience. Unlike desktop users who "browse," mobile users "hunt" and "scroll." The overarching theme for a high-converting mobile PDP is "Verticality & Discovery." Research consistently proves that mobile users are natural scrollers but reluctant tap-and-swipers. Therefore, the architecture of the page must minimize interaction cost (swiping/tapping) while maximizing "Information Scent." If a user cannot see it or easily scroll to it, it does not exist. The following guide details the scientifically proven layout to combat "banner blindness," reduce cognitive load, and guide the user from discovery to purchase without friction.

**Section 1: The Product Image Carousel (Top of Page)**

* **Primary Role (Browsing & Inspection):** This is the "Hook." Its psychological function is to bridge the gap between digital and physical. Since the user cannot touch the product, the carousel must simulate the physical examination experience. If this interaction fails (e.g., zoom is broken, scale is unclear), the user perceives the product as "low quality" or "risky," triggering immediate abandonment.

**1\. The "Dot" Problem vs. Visible Thumbnails**

* **The Mistake:** Using only "dots" (pagination indicators: • o o o o) to show there are more images.

* **The Baymard Finding:** **50-80% of users** overlook additional product images when only dots are used. Dots are too subtle for the mobile context where glare and thumb placement obscure them.

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Option A (Gold Standard):** Display **thumbnails** directly below the main image. Even on mobile, a row of small thumbnails encourages interaction significantly more than dots because users can *see* what they are missing (e.g., "Oh, there's a view of the back").

  * **Option B (The "Peek"):** If you want a cleaner look, use the **"Peek" method**. Ensure the main image doesn't span 100% of the screen width. Allow the edge of the *next* image to "peek" out from the right side. This visual disruption creates an irresistible affordance to swipe.

**2\. The "Pinch-to-Zoom" Mandate**

* **The Mistake:** Disabling pinch-to-zoom or relying solely on a "tap to zoom" icon.

* **The Baymard Finding:** **40% of mobile sites** fail to support pinch-to-zoom on product images. Users *instinctively* try to pinch to see details (texture, ports, ingredients). When it doesn't work, they perceive the site as "broken" or the product as "low quality."

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Support All Gestures:** You must support **Double-Tap** AND **Pinch**.

  * **High-Res Fetch:** When a user pinches, the site must dynamically swap the mobile-optimized image for a higher-resolution version. If a user pinches and the image just gets blurry (pixelated), conversion drops because they can't verify quality.

**3\. The "In Scale" & "Lifestyle" Requirement**

* **The Mistake:** Using only "Cut Out" images (product on pure white background).

* **The Baymard Finding:** On mobile, users lose the sense of scale. A toaster and a mini-fridge look the same size on a 6-inch screen.

* **The Best Practice:** You must include at least one **"In Scale" image**.

  * *Example:* If selling a bag, show it on a human model (to check strap length).

  * *Example:* If selling a supplement bottle, show it next to a common object (like a glass of water) or in a hand.

  * *Why:* This reduces returns and "hesitation" caused by users wondering, "Is this the travel size or the full size?"

**4\. The "Gallery Overlay"**

* **The Mistake:** Relying on the carousel for everything. The carousel is for *browsing*; the overlay is for *inspecting*.

* **The Best Practice:** Tapping the main image should open a **Full-Screen Gallery Overlay**.

  * **Features:** This overlay should have a dark background (to focus the eye), allow distinct left/right swiping, and support "deep zoom" (2x-4x).

  * **The "Back" Button Trap:** Ensure the "Close" button (X) is prominent. Baymard found that users often hit the browser "Back" button to close the image overlay, which accidentally takes them back to Google/Search results. You must "hijack" the back button behavior while the overlay is open so it only closes the image, not the page.

**5\. Video Integration**

* **The Mistake:** Hiding video in a separate "Video" section lower down the page.

* **The Best Practice:** Mix the video directly into the image carousel (usually as the last slide or the 2nd slide).

* **Visual Cue:** It must have a prominent "Play" icon overlay on the thumbnail so users know it's not just another photo.

**Section 2: The Product Overview (The "Buy Box")**

* **Primary Role (Identity & Selection):** This is the "Confirmation Zone." Its psychological goal is to reduce anxiety and confirm relevance. The user needs to verify they landed on the correct product variant (Identity) and perceive the value proposition immediately (Selection). This section fights "Confirmation Bias"—if the price or title is ambiguous, the user assumes the worst and leaves.

**1\. The Title: "Full Visibility" Rule**

* **The Mistake:** Truncating the product title with "..." after 1 or 2 lines to save space.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users rely on the title to confirm they landed on the correct specific variation (e.g., "Men's Vitamin" vs "Men's Vitamin *50+*"). Truncating creates anxiety.

* **The Best Practice:** **Always display the full product title.** If it takes 3 lines, let it take 3 lines. The clarity is worth the vertical height.

**2\. Review Stars: The "Anchor Link"**

* **The Mistake:** Static stars (visual only) or stars that open a pop-up modal.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users see the rating (e.g., 4.5 stars) and immediately want to verify *why* it’s not 5 stars.

* **The Best Practice:** The review summary (Stars \+ "(1,240 Reviews)") must be a **Page Anchor Link**. When tapped, it should smooth-scroll the user directly down to the Reviews section at the bottom of the page. This is a top-requested interaction that is often broken on mobile sites.

**3\. Price & Savings: "Do the Math"**

* **The Mistake:** Showing only the sale price, or just "$50 $̶6̶0̶".

* **The Baymard Finding:** Mobile users are often distracted. They don't do mental math well.

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Visual Hierarchy:** The current price must be significantly larger (font size) than the old price.

  * **Explicit Savings:** You must explicitly state **"Save $10"** or **"20% Off"** next to the price. This "calculated savings" triggers the loss-aversion psychology much faster than just seeing two numbers.

**4\. Variants (Flavors/Sizes): "Exposed" Selectors**

* **The Mistake:** Using a **Drop-Down Menu** for size, color, or flavor selection.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Drop-downs require 3+ taps (open, scroll/select, close) and hide the available options.

* **The Best Practice:** Use **"Exposed" Buttons (Chips)**.

  * *Rule:* If you have fewer than 10 options (e.g., 3 flavors), lay them out as visible buttons on the page.

  * *Why:* Users can instantly see "Oh, they have Chocolate" without interacting. It reduces friction by 60%.

  * *Out of Stock:* If a variant is sold out, do *not* hide it. Show it but "cross it out" or grey it out. Hiding it makes users think you never carried it.

**5\. "Auxiliary" Description (The Short Bullets)**

* **The Mistake:** Dumping the full paragraph description right here. It pushes the "Buy" button too far down.

* **The Best Practice:** Use a distinct **"Product Subtitle"** or **"Key Benefits" list**.

  * *Format:* 3–4 short bullet points max.

  * *Content:* This is for "Value Props," not "Specs." (e.g., "Clinically dosed," "Zero Sugar," "Made in USA").

  * *Function:* This confirms to the user *why* this product is special before they commit to scrolling down for the deep dive.

**6\. The "Shipping Proximity" Rule**

* **The Mistake:** Placing shipping info ("Free shipping on orders over $50") in the site-wide banner or the footer.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users determine "value" by combining **Price \+ Shipping**. If they can't see the shipping cost near the price, they assume it's expensive and hesitate.

* **The Best Practice:** Place a concise shipping summary directly below the price or the "Add to Cart" button.

  * *Example:* "$49.00 \- Free 2-Day Shipping"

**Section 3: The CTA & Checkout Section**

* **Primary Role (Commitment):** This is the "Friction Zone." Its psychological role is to facilitate action by removing "Cognitive Load" and "Risk." Every extra tap or ambiguous label here introduces doubt. The goal is to make the payment decision feel safe, easy, and reversible.

**1\. Subscription vs. One-Time Purchase (The "Default" Trap)**

* **The Mistake:** Pre-selecting "Subscribe & Save" without making it visually obvious, or hiding the "One-Time Purchase" option.

* **The Baymard Finding:** "Sneaking" a subscription into the cart is a top cause of cart abandonment and post-purchase chargebacks. Users feel tricked. However, pre-selecting subscription *can* work if the design is honest.

* **The Best Practice:** Use **"Selectable Tiles"** (Radio Buttons).

  * **Layout:** Stack two large, tappable tiles vertically:

    * $$ $$

**One-Time Purchase: $50**

* $$x$$

**Subscribe & Save 20%: $40** (Includes text: *"Cancel anytime"*)

* **Visuals:** The Subscription tile should be highlighted (e.g., a different background color or border) to show it is the active choice.

  * **Math:** Explicitly show the dollar amount saved inside the tile (e.g., "Save $10.00").

**2\. The Quantity Selector: "Stepper" vs. Dropdown**

* **The Mistake:** Using a native OS dropdown menu (scrolling wheel) for quantity.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Dropdowns are high-interaction cost (tap, scroll, done). For 90% of users, the desired quantity is "1".

* **The Best Practice:** Use a **"Stepper" (+ / \-)** interface.

  * **Design:** A simple \[ \- \] \[ 1 \] \[ \+ \] control.

  * **Hit Area:** Ensure the \- and \+ buttons are large enough (min 44x44 pixels) so users don't accidentally tap the wrong one.

**3\. The "Add to Cart" Button (The Holy Grail)**

* **The Mistake:** Small buttons, ghost buttons (transparent background), or "clever" text like "Get Started."

* **The Baymard Finding:**

  * **Width:** The button should span the **full width** of the container (comfortable for left or right thumbs).

  * **Label:** Use standard terminology: **"Add to Cart"** or **"Add to Bag."** Creative labels (e.g., "Start your Journey") increase cognitive load—users hesitate because they aren't sure if it adds to cart or goes to a quiz.

  * **State Change:** The button *must* visually react when tapped (change color or depress) to acknowledge the input.

**4\. Post-Click Behavior: "Stay" vs. "Go"**

* **The Mistake:** Redirecting the user immediately to the "Cart Page" after they click "Add to Cart."

* **The Baymard Finding:** This interrupts the shopping flow. If the user wanted to add a second bottle or read more reviews, you just forced them to navigate back (which is frustrating).

* **The Best Practice:** Use a **"Success Feedback" mechanism**.

  * **Option A (Slide-Out Cart):** The preferred modern method. A mini-cart slides in from the right, confirming the add and showing the total, but leaving the user on the PDP background.

  * **Option B (Toast Notification):** A temporary banner appears ("Added to Cart\!") with a "View Cart" link, but the user remains on the product page.

**5\. Third-Party Payments (The "Express" Buttons)**

* **The Mistake:** Cluttering the primary CTA area with 5 different buttons (PayPal, GPay, ShopPay, Apple Pay).

* **The Baymard Finding:** "Decision Paralysis." Too many colorful buttons compete with the main "Add to Cart" button.

* **The Best Practice:** Prioritize the **Primary Action**.

  * Place the main "Add to Cart" button first (prominent).

  * Place a single "More Payment Options" link or a simplified row of logos *below* the main button.

  * *Exception:* If you use a dynamic "Express Checkout" (like Shop Pay or Apple Pay), place it *under* the main ATC button with a "OR" divider text.

**6\. Micro-Copy (The "Anxiety Reducers")**

* **The Mistake:** Leaving the space around the button empty.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users have last-second anxieties right before tapping: "Is this secure?" "When will it ship?" "Can I return it?"

* **The Best Practice:** Add **"Micro-Trust Signals"** immediately below the button.

  * *Text:* "Secure Checkout • Ships Tomorrow • 30-Day Returns"

  * *Size:* Small font, neutral color (gray), centered.

  * *Why:* This creates a "safety net" for the click.

**Section 4: Product Details & Information**

* **Primary Role (Rationalization):** This is the "Logic Zone." The user has been emotionally hooked by the image and price; now they need logical proof to justify the purchase. The psychology here is "Information Scent"—users will only interact with content if they perceive a high probability that it contains the answer they need. Verticality is critical here to support the natural scanning behavior.

**1\. The Layout: "Accordion" vs. "Long Scroll"**

* **The Mistake:** Displaying the full description, full ingredient list, and full "How to Use" guide as open text blocks one after another.

* **The Baymard Finding:** This creates the "Endless Scroll." Users lose context of where they are on the page and can't easily skip to the section they care about (e.g., someone looking for "Allergens" doesn't want to scroll past 5 paragraphs of "Brand Story").

* **The Best Practice:** Use **Vertically Stacked Accordions** (Expandable Sections).

  * **Structure:**

    * **Description:** Open by default (or partially open with "Read More").

    * **Ingredients & Nutrition:** Collapsed (Header: "Ingredients \+").

    * **How to Use:** Collapsed (Header: "Directions \+").

    * **FAQs:** Collapsed.

  * **Why:** This gives the user a "Table of Contents" view. They can see all available topics at a glance and tap only what they need.

**2\. The "Description" Text: Feature vs. Benefit**

* **The Mistake:** Writing a "Wall of Text" (a single 10-line paragraph).

* **The Baymard Finding:** Mobile users do not read blocks of text; they scan. A dense paragraph causes "scanner's fatigue," and users often skip it entirely.

* **The Best Practice:** Break text into **"Snackable" Chunks**.

  * **Subheads:** Use bold subheaders every 2–3 sentences.

  * **Bullets:** Use bullet points for features.

  * **Icons:** Pair key benefits with simple icons (e.g., a "Leaf" icon next to "Plant-Based").

**3\. The "Hybrid" Winner: Truncation (The "Preview" Approach)**

* **The Context:** For a specific list like **Ingredients in a Formula**, Baymard strongly favors **Truncation** over fully hiding the content behind an accordion.

* **The Pattern:** Show the first 5–10 ingredients (or the first 3 lines of text) followed by a "View Full List" or "Read More" link.

* **Why it tests better:**

  1. **Maintains Scent:** The user immediately sees "Vitamin C, Zinc, Magnesium..." which confirms the *type* and *quality* of content hidden. They don't have to guess.

  2. **Saves Vertical Space:** It still solves the "long scroll" problem, protecting the path to the bottom of the page.

  3. **Reduces Interaction Cost:** Users scanning for a specific "dealbreaker" ingredient (e.g., sugar) might see it in the preview without ever needing to tap.

**4\. The "Ingredients" Technical Implementation**

* **The Mistake:** Using a low-resolution image of the "Supplement Facts" panel.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Images of text are a nightmare on mobile. They are often unreadable without pinching (which is annoying) and are not accessible to screen readers (bad for SEO and accessibility).

* **The Best Practice:** **HTML Text \+ "Label" Image**.

  * **Primary:** List the ingredients as real, selectable HTML text (using the "Truncation" method we discussed earlier).

  * **Secondary:** Provide a high-res image of the official "Supplement Facts" panel as a *link* or a tap-to-expand image for compliance/purists.

  * **Allergen Callout:** Distinctly separate allergens (e.g., "Contains: Soy") at the very top or bottom of this list in bold.

**5\. "Cross-Sell" Integration (The "Complete the Routine" Section)**

* **The Mistake:** Placing "You May Also Like" items randomly in the middle of the description.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Interrupting the user while they are reading about *Product A* to show them *Product B* increases distraction and reduces conversion for *Product A*.

* **The Best Practice:** Place Cross-Sells **below the Product Details** but **above Reviews**.

  * **Logic:** Once they finish reading the details, they have either decided to buy or decided "this isn't for me." That is the moment to offer an alternative.

  * **Bundling:** If it's a "Routine" (e.g., Shampoo \+ Conditioner), offer a "One-Click Bundle" button here.

**6\. The Vertical vs. Horizontal Consensus**

* **The Stat:** Users overlooked content hidden in **horizontal tabs 27% of the time**. In contrast, users overlooked content in **vertically collapsed sections only 8% of the time**.

* **The Reason:** Vertical scrolling is the primary, natural interaction on mobile. Users instinctively scroll down to find more information. Horizontal swiping (carousels) requires a conscious decision to stop scrolling and start swiping sideways. If the visual cues aren't perfect, users simply scroll past the carousel, never seeing the additional ingredients or details.

* **The Verdict:** Do NOT use horizontal carousels for primary decision-making content like ingredients. Use Vertical stacking (full list) or Vertical Accordions (expandable list).

**Section 5: The Dedicated FAQ Strategy**

* **Primary Role (Objection Handling):** This is the "Defense." Its psychological role is to pre-emptively resolve the top 5 barriers to purchase that your support team hears most often. Unlike the Description (which sells), the FAQ (clarifies).

**1\. The "Top 5" Rule (Curated vs. Dump)**

* **The Mistake:** Dumping the entire site-wide Help Center or 20+ questions onto the product page.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Mobile users suffer from information overload. They will not sift through 20 questions.

* **The Best Practice:** Curate the **Top 5-7 product-specific questions**.

  * *Source:* Ask your CS team: "What are the top 5 reasons people hesitate to buy *this specific item*?"

  * *Format:* If you have more than 7, use a "View all FAQs" link that opens a modal or new page.

**2\. The "No Marketing" Mandate**

* **The Mistake:** Disguising sales pitches as questions (e.g., *Q: "Why is this the world's best formula?"*).

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users identify this immediately as disingenuous. It lowers the credibility of the entire section.

* **The Best Practice:** Keep questions strictly functional and defensive. (e.g., *Q: "Does this contain caffeine?"*, *Q: "Is the bottle glass or plastic?"*, *Q: "Will this break my fast?"*).

**3\. Interaction Design: The Accordion Stack**

* **The Mistake:** Listing Questions and Answers as open text.

* **The Best Practice:** Use **Collapsed Accordions** by default.

  * **State:** All closed initially.

  * **Visuals:** High-contrast borders between items so touch targets are clear.

  * **Iconography:** Use a clear \+ on the right side to indicate expansion.

**4\. Contextual Linking**

* **The Mistake:** Writing 3 paragraphs to explain a complex Return Policy inside the FAQ accordion.

* **The Best Practice:** Give the "Headline Answer" (1 sentence) and link to the full policy.

  * *Example:* "Yes, we offer free returns within 30 days. \[Read our full Return Policy\]."

  * *Why:* This keeps the user on the PDP for the simple answer but provides an escape hatch for the complex detail without cluttering the mobile view.

**Section 6: Social Proof & Reviews**

* **Primary Role (Validation):** This is the "Truth Serum." Its psychological function is "Social Validation." Users trust other users more than the brand. At this stage of the scroll, the user is looking for reasons *not* to buy (dealbreakers). Making this content filterable and visual builds trust by proving there is nothing to hide.

**1\. The "Snapshot": The Histogram is Mandatory**

* **The Mistake:** Displaying just the average star rating (e.g., "4.5 Stars") without the breakdown.

* **The Baymard Finding:** A single number is insufficient. Users need to know the **Distribution**. Is it a "safe" 4.5 (mostly 5s and 4s) or a "controversial" 4.5 (lots of 5s but a worrying number of 1s)?

* **The Best Practice:** Display the **Review Histogram** (Bar Chart) prominently at the top of the section.

  * **Function:** Make the bars clickable/tappable. Tapping the "1-Star" bar should instantly filter the list to show those negative reviews. This is the \#1 interaction users want: "Show me why people hate this."

**2\. UGC Photos: The "Real World" Gallery**

* **The Mistake:** Burying user photos inside individual text reviews, forcing users to hunt for them.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users view customer photos as "unfiltered truth" to verify color, texture, and size. They want to see *all* of them at once.

* **The Best Practice:** Create a **"Customer Photo Strip"** or Grid immediately above the text reviews.

  * **The Overlay Rule:** When a user taps *one* customer photo, it must open a **Full-Screen Gallery Overlay** that lets them swipe through *all* customer photos from all reviews. Do not restrict them to just the photos from that single user.

**3\. Filtering: The "Search" Within Reviews**

* **The Mistake:** Relying only on "Sort by Date."

* **The Baymard Finding:** On mobile, scrolling through 500 reviews is impossible. Users need to find "reviews from people like me" or "reviews about my concern."

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Keyword Filters:** Offer tappable "chips" for common topics (e.g., "Taste," "Shipping," "Texture," "Result").

  * **Attribute Filters:** If you sell a formula, allow filtering by user type (e.g., "Age: 50+," "Golfer," "Sensitive Stomach"). This prevents a 20-year-old bodybuilder's review from confusing a 60-year-old golfer.

**4\. Loading Mechanics: "Load More" vs. Pagination**

* **The Mistake:** Using **Infinite Scroll** (reviews keep loading as you scroll) or **Pagination** (Page 1, 2, 3).

* **The Baymard Finding:**

  * **Infinite Scroll:** Terrible for reviews because it blocks access to the Footer. Users can never reach the bottom links (returns, contact).

  * **Pagination:** Hard to tap on mobile.

* **The Best Practice:** Use a large **"Load More" Button**.

  * **The Threshold:** Load **15–30 reviews** at a time. This balances performance with "scannability."

  * **Why:** It keeps the footer accessible while giving the user control over the experience.

**5\. The "Write a Review" Trap**

* **The Mistake:** Making the "Write a Review" button as prominent as the "Add to Cart" button.

* **The Baymard Finding:** You are selling to *buyers*, not recruiting writers. A giant "Write a Review" button distracts users who are there to purchase.

* **The Best Practice:** De-emphasize it.

  * Make it a secondary ghost button or a text link.

  * Place it *under* the Histogram, not next to the Price/Title.

**6\. Reviewer "Badges" (Trust Signals)**

* **The Mistake:** Anonymous reviews (e.g., "John S.").

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users are skeptical of fake reviews.

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Verified Buyer:** This badge is non-negotiable.

  * **"Real" Context:** Show the "Time Owned" (e.g., "Used for 3 months") if possible. This adds massive credibility compared to a review written 1 day after delivery.

**7\. The Individual Review Card Template**

* **Primary Role (Relevance):** To allow the user to quickly assess "Is this reviewer like me?" and "What is the core issue?" without reading a wall of text.

* **The "Hierarchy" Template:**

  * **Row 1 (The Hook):** **Star Rating** \+ **Bold Headline**. (Example: ★★★★★ **"Best sleep I've had in years"**). *Never* bury the headline.

  * **Row 2 (The Context):** **Reviewer Attributes** in gray text. (Example: *Age: 45-54 | Concern: Insomnia | Verified Buyer*).

  * **Row 3 (The Data):** **Pros/Cons Bullets**. Use green checks for Pros and red 'x's for Cons. This is 3x more readable than paragraph text.

  * **Row 4 (The Body):** The full text review.

* **The "Helpfulness" Signal:**

  * Include a "Was this review helpful?" (Thumb Up/Down) toggle.

  * **Why:** It crowdsources quality control, allowing the best reviews to float to the top automatically.

* **Merchant Response:**

  * If a review is 3 stars or less, display a **"Response from the Brand"** box directly below it.

  * **Tone:** Apologetic and solution-oriented. This proves to *prospective* buyers that you stand behind the product.

**Section 7: User-Generated Q\&A**

* **Primary Role (Gap Filling):** This is the "Safety Net." Its psychological function is to resolve "Specific Outliers." While reviews handle general sentiment, Q\&A handles specific blockers (e.g., "Can I take this while fasting?"). If a user has a specific question and can't find the answer, they will leave rather than email support.

**1\. The "Search First" Interface**

* **The Mistake:** Displaying a long, paginated list of random questions (e.g., "Does this ship to Canada?" next to "Is it vegan?").

* **The Baymard Finding:** Mobile users are looking for *their* specific question. They will not read 5 pages of unrelated queries.

* **The Best Practice:** Place a prominent **Keyword Search Field** ("Search questions...") at the top of this section. It should filter results instantly as the user types.

**2\. "Brand" vs. "Community" Distinction**

* **The Mistake:** Treating all answers visually the same.

* **The Baymard Finding:** Users look to the **Brand** for objective facts (safety, expiration) and **Other Users** for subjective experience (taste, routine). Confusion here damages trust.

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Brand Answers:** Must be visually distinct (e.g., specific background color, "Official Team" badge, logo).

  * **Community Answers:** Standard text styling.

**3\. The "2-Click" Exposure**

* **The Mistake:** Showing the full thread (Question \+ 5 Answers) by default. This consumes too much vertical pixel height.

* **The Best Practice:**

  * **Default State:** Show the **Question** \+ the **Top Answer** (truncated to 2 lines).

  * **Interaction:** Tap to expand the full thread or see alternative answers.

**4\. Zero-Result Handling**

* **The Mistake:** A prominent "Q\&A" header with "No questions yet" text.

* **The Baymard Finding:** This creates negative social proof (nobody is interested in this).

* **The Best Practice:** If the product has 0 questions, **hide the section entirely** or replace it with a direct "Ask a Question" link that goes privately to support, rather than a public empty board.

**Section 8: Persistent Navigation**

* **Primary Role (Retention & Wayfinding):** This is the "Safety Net." Its psychological goal is to prevent "Scroll Fatigue" and "Disorientation." As the user ventures deep into the content, they must always have a clear path back to the beginning (Context) or to the purchase (Conversion).

**1\. The "Sticky" ATC (Add-To-Cart)**

* **The Problem:** On long mobile pages, the main ATC button disappears as the user scrolls to read details/reviews.

* **The Fix:** Implement a **Sticky Footer CTA**. As soon as the main ATC button scrolls out of view, a compact bar should slide up from the bottom containing the product title, price, and a prominent "Add to Cart" button.

* **Baymard Stat:** This is critical because mobile pages are 4–10x longer than desktop pages. Don't make users scroll back up 10 screens to buy.

**2\. Breadcrumbs are Mandatory**

* **Location:** Top of the page (e.g., Home \> Men \> Shoes \> Running).

* **Function:** This is the \#1 way mobile users "back out" if they land on the wrong product from an ad. If you don't have breadcrumbs, they hit the browser "Back" button and leave your site entirely.

* **"Scope" Awareness:** When a user searches for "Red Shoes," keep them in that "scope." If they click a product and then hit "back," do not dump them on the Homepage. Return them to the "Red Shoes" search results at the exact scroll position they left.
